title: Pre-Asymptotic Trainability in Photonic Variational Circuits under Postselection
authors: Yichen Xie, Cassandre Notton, Jean Senellart
abstract: Barren plateaus in variational quantum circuits are commonly attributed to strong mixing dynamics that cause gradient variance to vanish exponentially with system size. Passive photonic circuits, central to linear optical quantum computing, challenge this picture: although their Hilbert space can be exponentially large, their dynamics are constrained to a Lie algebra whose dimension scales as the square of the number of modes. In photonic systems, postselection also plays a central role, with gradient concentration governed not by the Hilbert-space dimension but by how it reshapes the effective observable. Through exact statevector simulations, we compare allow-bunching evolution, collision-free filtering, and dual-rail postselection. In the allow-bunching and collision-free regimes, gradient variance remains consistent with polynomial rather than exponential decay over the tested system sizes. By contrast, dual-rail postselection induces exponential concentration beyond moderate system sizes, robustly across three initialization ensembles. These results indicate that photonic barren plateaus are governed by the interplay between passive linear-optical dynamics, postselection geometry, and task observables, offering practical guidance for designing near-term photonic variational architectures.
summary: This paper investigates why some photonic variational circuits suffer from Barren Plateaus while others do not. It finds that while dual-rail encoding leads to exponential gradient decay, other regimes like collision-free filtering maintain polynomial decay. This offers a practical guide for designing trainable near-term photonic quantum machine learning models.
main_problem: Investigating the presence of Barren Plateaus (exponentially vanishing gradients) in photonic variational quantum circuits and determining how different postselection regimes affect trainability.
main_result: Gradient variance decays polynomially in allow-bunching and collision-free regimes, but exhibits exponential decay (Barren Plateaus) in dual-rail postselection, showing that trainability is driven by how postselection reshapes effective observables.
method: The study uses exact statevector simulations and a Lie-algebraic framework to analyze gradient variance, comparing different postselection models and initialization ensembles.
model_or_system: Passive linear-optical quantum computing using programmable Mach-Zehnder Interferometer (MZI) meshes acting on n-photon Fock states in m optical modes.
key_observables: Gradient variance, g-purity, Bhattacharyya loss, and pulled-back observables.
important_parameters: Number of modes (m), number of photons (n), number of logical units (N), and postselection regimes (allow-bunching, collision-free, and dual-rail).
main_assumptions: Conclusions are based on finite-size numerical simulations; the theoretical framework uses a variance formula for linear observables while the experiment uses a nonlinear loss function.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows g-purity scaling; Figure 2 compares gradient variance across different postselection regimes, highlighting the separation between polynomial and exponential decay; Figure 3 shows the robustness of polynomial scaling to mode count; Figure 4 demonstrates a supervised learning task (MNIST) where the photonic model maintains higher gradient norms than the logical model.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Barren Plateau problem in photonics, defines the Lie-algebraic framework and postselection regimes, presents numerical scaling results for different regimes, demonstrates a practical machine learning application (MNIST), and discusses implications for circuit design.
why_interesting: It provides critical insights into the trainability of photonic quantum architectures, showing that unlike standard gate-based qubits, certain photonic postselection regimes can avoid the Barren Plateau problem by leveraging the specific Lie-algebraic structure of passive optics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1 is consistent with this mechanism at the level of g-purity estimates. The hierarchy P FOCK g (O) > P UNBUNCHED g (O) > P DUAL RAIL g (O) is observed across the tested mode counts, with roughly parallel slopes suggesting a systematic geometric effect of postselection independent of m.

Fig. 1: Pg(O) as a function of computation space for m ∈ {4, 6, 8, 10, 12} with n = m/2 photons. Stricter postselec- tion systematically decreases g-purity, consistent with code- induced barren plateaus. Marker size is proportional to sub- space dimension.

Fig. 2: Mean gradient variance bV (N) versus qubit count for all nine configurations, shown on log-linear axes and organized by initialization strategy. Each plotted point is estimated from 3000 nominal Monte Carlo samples, with one fresh random circuit and one fresh random target per sample. Circle area is proportional to postselected subspace dimension. Dashed lines indicate representative fitted trends. The purpose of this figure is to highlight the qualitative separation between the Fock and collision-free regimes and the dual-rail regime.

Fig. 3: Gradient variance versus total mode count m for fixed photon numbers N = 5–12 under collision-free (no- bunching) postselection, plotted on log-linear scales. Each curve corresponds to a fixed N. Straight lines on a log-linear plot indicate exponential scaling.

Fig. 4: Supervised learning demonstration: cross-entropy loss (top left), validation accuracy (top right), mean quantum gradi- ent norm (bottom left), and mean quantum gradient variance (bottom right) over 100 training epochs. Photonic (orange): 10-mode circuit with Fock-space output, no code postselec- tion. Logical (blue): 10-qubit gate-based circuit. Classical (green): MLP baseline. The photonic model maintains gradient norms and variances approximately one order of magnitude above the logical model throughout training, consistent with the absence of exponential gradient concentration in the no- code-postselection regime.
